content: What would you consider to be the signal-to-noise ratio of this video, which is livestreaming from mp4 to ascii? You might be able to identify a field of flowers blowing in the wind, intermittent cuts of the clapboard, or if you watch long enough, a purple convertible. But you must sift through the noise to find the signal that is the original movie [(source)](https://archive.org/details/200647_American_Choice_Outtakes_R6).
In machine-learning and statistical modeling, "signal" is the component of your data that carries **predictive information** about the quantity you're trying to estimate or classify.

Models take as input many features. Some carry more signal than others, and weighting them improves the overall ratio of signal to noise. But what is seen as high "signal" is in the eye of the beholder. For instance, if I were a beholder, I would say:
> The type of recommendation with the highest signal-to-noise ratio is someone I know well telling me "you should watch this, I think you'd like it".

Recommendation systems today simulate these core mechanisms. The system percieves me as a user, models the content, surfaces tailored recommendations, and retrains on my feedback. I try to be intentional with my feedback, to keep the [good movies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Soul_(2020_film)) coming.
But what happens when the system learns from signals [I don't even know I'm sending](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dwell_time_(information_retrieval))?
Platforms decide what your behavior means. Every like, pause, scroll, or comment becomes a feature or training data. But the system, not the user, decides which of these signals actually matter.

Popular recommendation systems which power YouTube, TikTok, Netflix, Instagram, Spotify, and more, shape the information we consume. Similarly, the food we eat shapes our physical health. That's why we have Nutrition Facts.

Nutrition Facts labels only became mandatory in the 1990s, after decades of rising health concerns and consumer pressure. Before that, food companies could say almost anything, and you had no standardized way to know what you were eating.

We can draw parallels from pre-1990s nutrition to many of our most beloved machine learning products. Recommendation systems today are both massively impactful and almost always more opaque than not. We rarely know all of the incentives and optimization targets behind the feeds we consume. Perhaps individual pieces of content, or models, or algorithms, should come along with **Nutrition Facts**.

["Show me less of this"](https://support.google.com/youtube/answer/6342839?hl=en&co=GENIE.Platform%3DAndroid) is a common feature in many recommendation systems for controlling your feed, but it's sparingly clicked by users. Bluesky is doing [interesting work](https://bsky.social/about/blog/3-30-2023-algorithmic-choice) in the space of transparent and decentralized recommendation systems.

Cutting edge model architectures have grown to satisfy recommendation at massive scale. Uber, [Instagram Explore](https://engineering.fb.com/2023/08/09/ml-applications/scaling-instagram-explore-recommendations-system/), and many others adopted the two-towers model architecture just a few years ago. And [it turns out](https://arxiv.org/abs/1905.06874), the sequential nature of transformers lends itself well to ranking. And recommenders of the next decade appear to be trending towards conversational interfaces, with Anthropic and OpenAI doubling down on the "bigger is better" approach - throwing [eye-watering sums of cash](https://openai.com/index/announcing-the-stargate-project/) into pretraining colossal generative models. An extension of transformers, [Mixture of Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/1701.06538), has gained strong traction among companies training frontier models.

Despite these advances, recommendation systems remain opaque. Engineers can trace signals, tweak hyperparameters, and watch drift in dashboards, but this level of control is rarely surfaced to the end user.

content: I often hear the argument that users cannot understand the technical intricacies of machine learning. But it doesn't make it any easier to understand systems when they're fundamentally opaque. Similar to how nutrition facts don't require a PhD in biochemistry to read, recommenders don't require a PhD in deep learning to understand.

It sure would be nice to see a high-resolution view of all the various optimization targets behind the system. And users should have an idea of the cause and effect of app interactions - if DMing photos of Magic the Gathering cards to my friends results in my feed being smothered in MTG content, then my choice is clear - I'll be DMing my friends more photos of MTG cards. And finally, we need to provide to users more explicit customizability features over the algorithm, passing the joystick from the engineers to the users. In opaque systems built on inferred behavioral signals, this may be the clearest signal the model will ever receive.
We are the experts in our own taste, after all.

By now I hope to have convinced you that ML understandability is an important problem, and that there's a tremendous amount of greenfield experimentation left to do in this space. And now, we're motivated to understand the _how_.

The **[Two Towers](https://engineering.fb.com/2023/08/09/ml-applications/scaling-instagram-explore-recommendations-system/)** architecture splits retrieval into two independent encoders: one for the user, one for the item; each of which produces a dense embedding. We then can take the dot product between the two embeddings to compute similarity between users (e.g. scroller of instagram) and content (e.g. instagram posts). The main efficiency here comes from the ability to (mostly) precompute both embeddings offline and indexing them with something like HNSW, pgvector, or FAISS. Two Towers shines at scale - given a user embedding, searching over millions to billions of precomputed content embeddings.

```
 user signals          item metadata
     │                      │
 [user tower]          [item tower]
     │                      │
 [embedding] ──── · ──── [embedding]
                  │
           similarity score
              → top-k candidates
```

**BST ([Alibaba, 2019](https://arxiv.org/abs/1905.06874))** applies transformer self-attention across a user's ordered interaction history rather than averaging it. The model learns which past items are most relevant to the current moment and encodes *time offset* from the target item instead of absolute position.

```
 item₋₄   item₋₃   item₋₂   item₋₁   target
 (Δt=−4d) (Δt=−3d) (Δt=−1d) (Δt=−2h)   │
   │         │         │         │        │
   └─────────┴────── attention ──┴────────┘
                         │
                   user embedding
```

**Mixture of Experts ([Shazeer et al., 2017](https://arxiv.org/abs/1701.06538))** replaces a single feedforward network with N specialized experts and a gating network that routes each token to only the top-k of them. This way, most of the model is idle on any given input. You get a much larger effective model for roughly the same compute cost per forward pass.

```
          input token
               │
        [gating network]
       ┌───┬───┴───┬───┐
     FFN₁ FFN₂  FFN₃ FFN₄   ← N experts
       ✗    ✓     ✓    ✗    ← top-2 selected
            │     │
       [weighted sum]
            │
          output
```
